1→# mise: Python Ecosystem
     2→
     3→Python version management, virtual environment handling, and common task patterns for Python projects.
     4→
     5→## Python Version Management
     6→
     7→mise manages Python versions through its core plugin. No separate plugin install required.
     8→
     9→```toml
    10→[tools]
    11→python = "3.12"               # Latest 3.12.x (prefix match)
    12→```
    13→
    14→Other version formats:
    15→
    16→```toml
    17→[tools]
    18→python = "3.12.4"             # Exact version
    19→# or
    20→python = ["3.11", "3.12"]     # Multiple versions (first is default)
    21→```
    22→
    23→By default, mise installs precompiled Python binaries for speed. To compile from source (needed for custom build flags or uncommon platforms):
    24→
    25→```toml
    26→[settings]
    27→python.compile = true
    28→```
    29→
    30→### Idiomatic version files
    31→
    32→mise reads `.python-version` files when idiomatic version file support is enabled, providing compatibility with pyenv-based workflows.
    33→
    34→## Virtual Environments
    35→
    36→Configure automatic venv creation and activation through the `[env]` section:
    37→
    38→```toml
    39→[env]
    40→_.python.venv = { path = ".venv", create = true }
    41→```
    42→
    43→This creates `.venv` if it doesn't exist and activates it when entering the project directory.
    44→
    45→### Extended options
    46→
    47→```toml
    48→[env]
    49→_.python.venv = {
    50→  path = ".venv",
    51→  create = true,
    52→  python = "3.11",                          # Pin venv to specific version
    53→  uv_create_args = ["--seed"]               # Pass args to uv venv
    54→}
    55→```
    56→
    57→### UV integration
    58→
    59→To align UV's Python resolution with the mise-managed version:
    60→
    61→```toml
    62→[env]
    63→UV_PYTHON = { value = "{{ tools.python.path }}", tools = true }
    64→```
    65→
    66→### Settings
    67→
    68→```toml
    69→[settings]
    70→python.uv_venv_auto = false                # false | "source" | "create|source" | true
    71→python.venv_stdlib = false                  # Use stdlib venv instead of uv/virtualenv
    72→```
    73→
    74→## uv Integration
    75→
    76→Most Python projects use uv as the package manager. Prefix commands with `uv run` to execute through the managed venv:
    77→
    78→```toml
    79→[tasks.python-install]
    80→description = "Install Python dependencies"
    81→run = "uv sync"
    82→
    83→[tasks.python-upgrade]
    84→description = "Upgrade Python dependencies"
    85→run = "uv lock --upgrade"
    86→
    87→[tasks.python-clean]
    88→description = "Clean development environment"
    89→run = ["rm -rf .venv", "rm -rf requirements.lock"]
    90→```
    91→
    92→For CI, use `uv sync --frozen` to install from the lockfile without updating it.
    93→
    94→## Task Patterns
    95→
    96→All task commands below assume uv-managed projects. Prefix with `uv run` when executing tools installed in the project venv.
    97→
    98→### Testing
    99→
   100→```toml
   101→[tasks.test]
   102→description = "Run test suite"
   103→run = "uv run pytest"
   104→
   105→[tasks."test:coverage"]
   106→description = "Run tests with coverage"
   107→run = "uv run pytest --cov=src --cov-report=term-missing"
   108→
   109→[tasks."test:watch"]
   110→description = "Run tests on file change"
   111→run = "uv run ptw -- -x"
   112→```
   113→
   114→### Linting and Formatting
   115→
   116→```toml
   117→[tasks.lint]
   118→description = "Check code style"
   119→run = "uv run ruff check ."
   120→
   121→[tasks."lint:fix"]
   122→description = "Auto-fix lint issues"
   123→run = "uv run ruff check --fix ."
   124→
   125→[tasks.format]
   126→description = "Format code"
   127→run = "uv run ruff format ."
   128→
   129→[tasks."format:check"]
   130→description = "Check formatting without changes"
   131→run = "uv run ruff format --check ."
   132→```
   133→
   134→### Type Checking
   135→
   136→```toml
   137→[tasks.typecheck]
   138→description = "Run type checker"
   139→run = "uv run mypy src/"
   140→```
   141→
   142→### Quality Gate
   143→
   144→```toml
   145→[tasks.check]
   146→description = "Full quality gate"
   147→depends = ["format:check", "lint", "typecheck", "test"]
   148→```
   149→
   150→### Packaging
   151→
   152→```toml
   153→[tasks.build]
   154→description = "Build distribution packages"
   155→depends = ["check"]
   156→run = "uv run python -m build"
   157→
   158→[tasks.publish]
   159→description = "Publish to PyPI"
   160→depends = ["build"]
   161→run = "uv run twine upload dist/*"
   162→```
   163→
   164→### Development Server
   165→
   166→```toml
   167→[tasks.serve]
   168→description = "Run development server"
   169→run = "uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000"
   170→
   171→[tasks."db:migrate"]
   172→description = "Run database migrations"
   173→run = "uv run alembic upgrade head"
   174→
   175→[tasks."db:revision"]
   176→description = "Create new migration"
   177→usage = 'arg "<message>" help="Migration description"'
   178→run = "uv run alembic revision --autogenerate -m \"$1\""
   179→```
   180→
   181→## Default Packages
   182→
   183→Create `~/.default-python-packages` with one package per line to auto-install into every new Python version:
   184→
   185→```text
   186→pip
   187→setuptools
   188→wheel
   189→pipx
   190→```
   191→
   192→Set `MISE_PYTHON_DEFAULT_PACKAGES_FILE` to use a custom path.
   193→

1→# mise: Rust Ecosystem
     2→
     3→Rust toolchain management and common task patterns for Rust projects.
     4→
     5→## Rust Toolchain Management
     6→
     7→mise manages Rust through rustup. Installations live in rustup's directory structure, not mise's. mise sets `RUSTUP_TOOLCHAIN` to activate the correct version.
     8→
     9→```toml
    10→[tools]
    11→rust = "stable"               # Most common
    12→```
    13→
    14→Other channel options:
    15→
    16→```toml
    17→[tools]
    18→rust = "1.83"                 # Pinned version
    19→# or
    20→rust = "nightly"              # Nightly channel
    21→```
    22→
    23→### Components and profiles
    24→
    25→```toml
    26→[tools]
    27→rust = {
    28→  version = "stable",
    29→  components = "clippy,rustfmt,rust-src",
    30→  profile = "default"
    31→}
    32→```
    33→
    34→Profile options: `minimal` (compiler only), `default` (plus docs, rustfmt, clippy), `complete` (all components).
    35→
    36→### Cross-compilation targets
    37→
    38→```toml
    39→[tools]
    40→rust = {
    41→  version = "stable",
    42→  targets = "wasm32-unknown-unknown,aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu"
    43→}
    44→```
    45→
    46→### When to use mise vs rustup directly
    47→
    48→Use mise for version pinning in `mise.toml` — the project declares which Rust version it needs, and mise activates it. Rustup handles the underlying installation, component management, and target configuration. The two work together: mise selects, rustup provides.
    49→
    50→### Rust settings
    51→
    52→```toml
    53→[settings]
    54→rust.cargo_home = "~/.cargo"
    55→rust.rustup_home = "~/.rustup"
    56→```
    57→
    58→These isolate mise's Rust installation from other rustup setups on the system when needed.
    59→
    60→## Task Patterns
    61→
    62→### Building
    63→
    64→```toml
    65→[tasks.build]
    66→description = "Build in debug mode"
    67→run = "cargo build"
    68→
    69→[tasks."build:release"]
    70→description = "Build optimized release binary"
    71→run = "cargo build --release"
    72→sources = ["src/**/*.rs", "Cargo.toml", "Cargo.lock"]
    73→outputs = ["target/release/{{env.CARGO_BIN_NAME}}"]
    74→```
    75→
    76→### Testing
    77→
    78→```toml
    79→[tasks.test]
    80→description = "Run test suite"
    81→run = "cargo test"
    82→
    83→[tasks."test:verbose"]
    84→description = "Run tests with output"
    85→run = "cargo test -- --nocapture"
    86→
    87→[tasks."test:doc"]
    88→description = "Run documentation tests"
    89→run = "cargo test --doc"
    90→```
    91→
    92→### Linting and Formatting
    93→
    94→```toml
    95→[tasks.lint]
    96→description = "Run clippy with strict warnings"
    97→run = "cargo clippy -- -D warnings"
    98→
    99→[tasks.format]
   100→description = "Format code"
   101→run = "cargo fmt"
   102→
   103→[tasks."format:check"]
   104→description = "Check formatting without changes"
   105→run = "cargo fmt -- --check"
   106→```
   107→
   108→### Quality Gate
   109→
   110→```toml
   111→[tasks.check]
   112→description = "Full quality gate"
   113→depends = ["format:check", "lint", "test"]
   114→```
   115→
   116→### Documentation
   117→
   118→```toml
   119→[tasks.doc]
   120→description = "Build and open docs"
   121→run = "cargo doc --open --no-deps"
   122→
   123→[tasks."doc:check"]
   124→description = "Check doc warnings"
   125→run = "RUSTDOCFLAGS='-D warnings' cargo doc --no-deps"
   126→```
   127→
   128→### Development Workflow
   129→
   130→```toml
   131→[tasks.watch]
   132→description = "Rebuild on file changes"
   133→run = "cargo watch -x build"
   134→
   135→[tasks.run]
   136→description = "Run the binary"
   137→depends = ["build"]
   138→run = "cargo run"
   139→
   140→[tasks.install]
   141→description = "Install binary locally"
   142→run = "cargo install --path ."
   143→```
   144→
   145→### Workspace Tasks
   146→
   147→For Cargo workspaces with multiple crates:
   148→
   149→```toml
   150→[tasks."ws:test"]
   151→description = "Test all workspace members"
   152→run = "cargo test --workspace"
   153→
   154→[tasks."ws:check"]
   155→description = "Check all workspace members"
   156→run = "cargo check --workspace"
   157→```
   158→
   159→## Cross-Compilation
   160→
   161→Set target-specific environment variables through `[env]`:
   162→
   163→```toml
   164→[env]
   165→CARGO_TARGET_AARCH64_UNKNOWN_LINUX_GNU_LINKER = "aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc"
   166→
   167→[tasks."build:arm64"]
   168→description = "Cross-compile for ARM64 Linux"
   169→run = "cargo build --release --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu"
   170→```
   171→
   172→For WASM targets:
   173→
   174→```toml
   175→[tasks."build:wasm"]
   176→description = "Build for WASM"
   177→run = "cargo build --release --target wasm32-unknown-unknown"
   178→```
   179→
   180→## Cargo Tool Extensions
   181→
   182→Install additional Cargo tools through mise backends:
   183→
   184→```toml
   185→[tools]
   186→"cargo:cargo-watch" = "latest"
   187→"cargo:cargo-nextest" = "latest"
   188→"cargo:cargo-audit" = "latest"
   189→```
   190→
   191→These install as mise-managed tools, separate from the project's `Cargo.toml` dependencies.
   192→

1→# mise: Examples
     2→
     3→Complete `mise.toml` files for common project types. Copy and adapt as needed.
     4→
     5→## Common Base Tasks
     6→
     7→Standard boilerplate tasks that appear across most projects. Include the relevant subset when onboarding a repo.
     8→
     9→```toml
    10→min_version = "2024.9.5"
    11→
    12→[settings]
    13→auto_install = true
    14→not_found_auto_install = true
    15→task_run_auto_install = true
    16→
    17→[settings.status]
    18→show_tools = true
    19→show_env = false
    20→truncate = true
    21→
    22→[tools]
    23→git-cliff = "2.10.1"
    24→prek = "0.2.20"
    25→"pipx:rumdl" = "latest"
    26→
    27→# === Changelog ===
    28→[tasks.changelog]
    29→description = "Update changelog using git-cliff"
    30→run = "git-cliff -o CHANGELOG.md"
    31→
    32→# === Hooks ===
    33→[tasks.hooks-install]
    34→description = "Install pre-commit and infisical hooks"
    35→run = "prek install -f && infisical scan install --pre-commit-hook"
    36→
    37→[tasks.pre-commit-run]
    38→description = "Run pre-commit hooks"
    39→run = "prek run --all-files"
    40→
    41→# === Security ===
    42→[tasks.infisical-scan]
    43→description = "Scan repository for secrets"
    44→run = "infisical scan"
    45→
    46→# === Markdown ===
    47→[tasks.markdown-lint]
    48→description = "Lint Markdown files"
    49→run = "rumdl check ."
    50→
    51→[tasks.markdown-fix]
    52→description = "Fix Markdown files"
    53→run = "rumdl fmt ."
    54→
    55→# === Composite ===
    56→[tasks.setup]
    57→description = "Set up development environment"
    58→depends = ["hooks-install"]
    59→```
    60→
    61→## Minimal Starter
    62→
    63→Bare-minimum configuration for any project. Add tools and tasks as the project grows.
    64→
    65→```toml
    66→[tools]
    67→node = "22"
    68→
    69→[tasks]
    70→dev = "npm run dev"
    71→test = "npm test"
    72→```
    73→
    74→## Python Web Application
    75→
    76→Full configuration for a Python web project with FastAPI, database migrations, and standard quality tooling.
    77→
    78→```toml
    79→[env]
    80→DATABASE_URL = "postgres://localhost/myapp_dev"
    81→_.python.venv = { path = ".venv", create = true }
    82→
    83→[tools]
    84→python = "3.12"
    85→
    86→[tasks]
    87→serve.run = "uvicorn app.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000"
    88→serve.description = "Run development server"
    89→
    90→test.run = "pytest"
    91→test.description = "Run test suite"
    92→
    93→"test:coverage".run = "pytest --cov=src --cov-report=term-missing"
    94→"test:coverage".description = "Run tests with coverage"
    95→
    96→lint.run = "ruff check ."
    97→lint.description = "Check code style"
    98→
    99→"lint:fix".run = "ruff check --fix ."
   100→format.run = "ruff format ."
   101→typecheck.run = "mypy src/"
   102→
   103→[tasks.check]
   104→description = "Full quality gate"
   105→depends = ["lint", "typecheck", "test"]
   106→
   107→[tasks."db:migrate"]
   108→description = "Run database migrations"
   109→run = "alembic upgrade head"
   110→
   111→[tasks."db:revision"]
   112→description = "Create new migration"
   113→usage = 'arg "<message>" help="Migration description"'
   114→run = "alembic revision --autogenerate -m \"$1\""
   115→
   116→[settings]
   117→experimental = true
   118→```
   119→
   120→## Rust CLI Tool
   121→
   122→Configuration for a Rust CLI project with standard Cargo workflows and source tracking.
   123→
   124→```toml
   125→[env]
   126→RUST_LOG = "info"
   127→
   128→[tools]
   129→rust = { version = "stable", components = "clippy,rustfmt" }
   130→"cargo:cargo-watch" = "latest"
   131→
   132→[tasks]
   133→build.run = "cargo build"
   134→build.description = "Build in debug mode"
   135→
   136→"build:release".run = "cargo build --release"
   137→"build:release".description = "Build optimized release binary"
   138→"build:release".sources = ["src/**/*.rs", "Cargo.toml", "Cargo.lock"]
   139→
   140→test.run = "cargo test"
   141→test.description = "Run test suite"
   142→
   143→lint.run = "cargo clippy -- -D warnings"
   144→lint.description = "Run clippy with strict warnings"
   145→
   146→format.run = "cargo fmt"
   147→"format:check".run = "cargo fmt -- --check"
   148→
   149→watch.run = "cargo watch -x build"
   150→watch.description = "Rebuild on file changes"
   151→
   152→run.run = "cargo run"
   153→run.depends = ["build"]
   154→
   155→install.run = "cargo install --path ."
   156→
   157→[tasks.check]
   158→description = "Full quality gate"
   159→depends = ["format:check", "lint", "test"]
   160→
   161→[settings]
   162→experimental = true
   163→```
   164→
   165→## Multi-Language Project
   166→
   167→Python backend with Node.js tooling (documentation site, pre-commit hooks, or frontend build).
   168→
   169→```toml
   170→[env]
   171→_.python.venv = { path = ".venv", create = true }
   172→
   173→[tools]
   174→python = "3.12"
   175→node = "22"
   176→
   177→[tasks]
   178→test.run = "pytest"
   179→lint.run = "ruff check . && eslint docs/"
   180→format.run = ["ruff format .", "prettier --write docs/"]
   181→
   182→"docs:dev".run = "npm run docs:dev"
   183→"docs:dev".description = "Run docs development server"
   184→
   185→"docs:build".run = "npm run docs:build"
   186→"docs:build".description = "Build documentation site"
   187→
   188→[tasks.check]
   189→description = "Full quality gate"
   190→depends = ["lint", "test", "docs:build"]
   191→
   192→[settings]
   193→experimental = true
   194→```
   195→
   196→## Infrastructure as Code (Terraform)
   197→
   198→Configuration for a Terraform project with environment-scoped validation, linting, and operations. Demonstrates `dir` for targeting subdirectories.
   199→
   200→```toml
   201→[settings]
   202→auto_install = true
   203→not_found_auto_install = true
   204→jobs = 4
   205→
   206→[settings.status]
   207→show_tools = true
   208→
   209→[tools]
   210→terraform = "1.14"
   211→terraform-docs = "0.21"
   212→tflint = "0.61"
   213→yamllint = "1.38"
   214→yamlfmt = "0.21"
   215→shellcheck = "0.11"
   216→
   217→[tasks.fmt]
   218→description = "Format all Terraform files recursively"
   219→dir = "infrastructure"
   220→run = "terraform fmt -recursive"
   221→
   222→[tasks.fmt-check]
   223→description = "Check formatting (CI-friendly)"
   224→dir = "infrastructure"
   225→run = "terraform fmt -recursive -check -diff"
   226→
   227→[tasks.yaml-fmt]
   228→description = "Format YAML files"
   229→run = "yamlfmt ."
   230→
   231→[tasks.prod-validate]
   232→description = "Validate production Terraform"
   233→dir = "infrastructure/environments/production"
   234→run = "terraform init -backend=false -input=false >/dev/null && terraform validate"
   235→
   236→[tasks.prod-plan]
   237→description = "Plan production changes"
   238→dir = "infrastructure/environments/production"
   239→run = "terraform plan"
   240→
   241→[tasks.prod-apply]
   242→description = "Apply production changes"
   243→dir = "infrastructure/environments/production"
   244→run = "terraform apply"
   245→
   246→[tasks.lint-prod]
   247→description = "Run TFLint in production"
   248→dir = "infrastructure/environments/production"
   249→run = "tflint --init && tflint"
   250→
   251→[tasks.shellcheck]
   252→description = "Lint shell scripts"
   253→run = "find scripts -name '*.sh' -exec shellcheck {} +"
   254→
   255→[tasks.docs]
   256→description = "Generate Terraform documentation"
   257→run = "./scripts/generate-docs.sh"
   258→
   259→[tasks.check]
   260→description = "Format, lint, and validate"
   261→depends = ["fmt", "lint-prod", "prod-validate"]
   262→env = { TF_IN_AUTOMATION = "true", TF_INPUT = "false" }
   263→
   264→[tasks.full-check]
   265→description = "Complete validation suite"
   266→depends = ["fmt", "yaml-fmt", "prod-validate", "lint-prod", "shellcheck", "docs"]
   267→env = { TF_IN_AUTOMATION = "true", TF_INPUT = "false" }
   268→
   269→[tasks.setup]
   270→description = "Initial project setup"
   271→depends = ["docs"]
   272→```
   273→
   274→## Claude Code Integration
   275→
   276→Tasks that wrap Claude Code CLI for AI-assisted workflows. Add to a project's `mise.toml` or a global `~/.config/mise/config.toml`.
   277→
   278→```toml
   279→[tasks."cc:ask"]
   280→description = "One-shot question to Claude about this project"
   281→run = "claude --print \"$1\""
   282→
   283→[tasks."cc:review"]
   284→description = "AI review of staged changes"
   285→run = "git diff --staged | claude --print 'Review these changes for bugs, style issues, and security concerns.'"
   286→
   287→[tasks."cc:explain"]
   288→description = "Explain a file using Claude"
   289→usage = 'arg "<file>" help="File to explain"'
   290→run = "cat \"$1\" | claude --print 'Explain this file. Focus on architecture and key decisions.'"
   291→
   292→[settings]
   293→experimental = true
   294→```
   295→
   296→For global cross-repo tasks, place in `~/.config/mise/config.toml`:
   297→
   298→```toml
   299→[tasks."repo:status"]
   300→description = "Show git state of all managed repos"
   301→run = '''
   302→for repo in ~/dev/projects/*/; do
   303→  if [ -d "$repo/.git" ]; then
   304→    printf "%-30s %s\n" "$(basename $repo)" "$(git -C $repo status --short | head -1)"
   305→  fi
   306→done
   307→'''
   308→```

1→# repo-forge
     2→
     3→Project setup and validation toolkit. Ensures repos are properly configured
     4→with standard tooling across Python, Rust, Terraform, and Ansible projects.
     5→
     6→## Skills
     7→
     8→| Skill | Status | Purpose |
     9→|-------|--------|---------|
    10→| `mise` | Available | Config patterns, task definitions, language-specific setup |
    11→| `pre-commit` | Planned | Hook configuration and repo setup |
    12→| `gitignore` | Planned | Template generation and validation per project type |
    13→| `git-cliff` | Planned | Changelog config and conventional commit conventions |
    14→| `project-setup` | Planned | Project type identification and orchestrated setup |
    15→

1→---
     2→name: mise
     3→description: >-
     4→  This skill provides mise configuration patterns, task definitions, and language-specific
     5→  setup guidance. This skill should be used when the user asks to "set up mise", "create a
     6→  mise.toml", "configure task runner", "manage tool versions", "add a task to mise",
     7→  "set up Python with mise", "configure Rust toolchain", "onboard a repo", "add mise to this
     8→  project", "set up dev environment", or mentions mise configuration, dev tooling setup,
     9→  task definitions, or project onboarding.
    10→---
    11→
    12→# mise
    13→
    14→mise is a polyglot tool version manager and task runner. A single `mise.toml` replaces Makefiles, shell scripts, and language-specific version managers (nvm, pyenv, rustup). It manages tool versions, defines project tasks, and sets environment variables from one configuration file.
    15→
    16→## Config File Structure
    17→
    18→Order sections canonically for consistency:
    19→
    20→```toml
    21→min_version = "2024.9.5"
    22→
    23→[env]
    24→DATABASE_URL = "postgres://localhost/myapp_dev"
    25→
    26→[tools]
    27→python = "3.12"
    28→
    29→[tasks]
    30→test.run = "pytest"
    31→
    32→[settings]
    33→auto_install = true
    34→not_found_auto_install = true
    35→task_run_auto_install = true
    36→```
    37→
    38→Use `min_version` to enforce a minimum mise version. Recognized top-level sections: `[env]`, `[tools]`, `[tasks]`, `[settings]`, `[plugins]`, `[tool_alias]`, `[task_config]`, `[vars]`.
    39→
    40→## Tool Version Management
    41→
    42→Specify tools in the `[tools]` section. mise installs and activates them automatically.
    43→
    44→```toml
    45→[tools]
    46→node = "22"                   # Major version prefix
    47→python = "3.12.2"             # Exact version
    48→go = "latest"                 # Latest stable
    49→erlang = "lts"                # LTS channel
    50→```
    51→
    52→Multiple versions install side-by-side (first is default):
    53→
    54→```toml
    55→[tools]
    56→python = ["3.11", "3.12"]
    57→```
    58→
    59→### Version formats
    60→
    61→| Format | Example | Behavior |
    62→|--------|---------|----------|
    63→| Exact | `"3.12.2"` | Specific version |
    64→| Prefix | `"3.12"` | Latest matching 3.12.x |
    65→| Channel | `"latest"`, `"lts"` | Resolved at install time |
    66→| Reference | `"ref:main"` | Git ref, compiled from source |
    67→| Subtraction | `"sub-1:latest"` | One major behind latest |
    68→
    69→### Table form with options
    70→
    71→```toml
    72→[tools]
    73→node = { version = "22", postinstall = "corepack enable" }
    74→rust = { version = "stable", components = "clippy,rustfmt", profile = "default" }
    75→```
    76→
    77→### Backend syntax for non-core tools
    78→
    79→```toml
    80→[tools]
    81→"ubi:astral-sh/ruff" = "latest"     # GitHub binary releases
    82→"cargo:cargo-watch" = "latest"       # Cargo crate
    83→"npm:prettier" = "3"                 # npm package
    84→"pipx:ansible-lint" = "latest"       # Python CLI tools via pipx
    85→```
    86→
    87→### Custom plugins
    88→
    89→Register custom tool plugins in `[plugins]`:
    90→
    91→```toml
    92→[plugins]
    93→fnox-env = "https://github.com/jdx/mise-env-fnox"
    94→```
    95→
    96→## Task Definitions
    97→
    98→Tasks are defined in `[tasks]` or as executable files in `.mise/tasks/`.
    99→
   100→### Inline tasks
   101→
   102→```toml
   103→tasks.test = "pytest"
   104→tasks.lint = "ruff check ."
   105→tasks.format = "ruff format ."
   106→```
   107→
   108→### Table tasks
   109→
   110→```toml
   111→[tasks.build]
   112→description = "Build the project"
   113→depends = ["lint"]
   114→run = "cargo build --release"
   115→dir = "{{config_root}}/backend"
   116→env = { RUST_LOG = "info" }
   117→sources = ["src/**/*.rs", "Cargo.toml"]
   118→outputs = ["target/release/myapp"]
   119→```
   120→
   121→### Task-level options
   122→
   123→Pin tool versions per-task, set aliases for shortcuts, or override the shell:
   124→
   125→```toml
   126→[tasks."lint:shellcheck"]
   127→description = "Lint shell scripts"
   128→run = "shellcheck scripts/*.sh"
   129→tools = { "shellcheck" = "0.11.0" }    # Pin tool version for this task
   130→alias = "sc"                            # Short alias for mise run sc
   131→shell = "bash -c"                       # Override default shell
   132→```
   133→
   134→### Task directory scoping
   135→
   136→Use `dir` to run a task in a specific subdirectory. Essential for monorepos and infrastructure projects where different tasks target different paths:
   137→
   138→```toml
   139→[tasks.prod-validate]
   140→description = "Validate production Terraform"
   141→dir = "infrastructure/environments/production"
   142→run = "terraform init -backend=false -input=false >/dev/null && terraform validate"
   143→
   144→[tasks.prod-plan]
   145→description = "Plan production changes"
   146→dir = "infrastructure/environments/production"
   147→run = "terraform plan"
   148→```
   149→
   150→Relative paths resolve from the config file's directory. Use `{{config_root}}` for explicit anchoring. When multiple tasks share a base directory, the pattern of environment-scoped tasks (same command, different `dir`) keeps configs DRY while remaining explicit about scope.
   151→
   152→### Multi-line scripts
   153→
   154→```toml
   155→[tasks.setup]
   156→run = [
   157→  "pip install -e '.[dev]'",
   158→  "pre-commit install"
   159→]
   160→```
   161→
   162→### File-based tasks
   163→
   164→Place executable scripts in `.mise/tasks/`. Subdirectories create namespaces — `.mise/tasks/db/migrate` becomes task `db:migrate`.
   165→
   166→```bash
   167→#!/usr/bin/env bash
   168→#MISE description="Run database migrations"
   169→#MISE depends=["db:check"]
   170→
   171→alembic upgrade head
   172→```
   173→
   174→Mark file tasks executable (`chmod +x`). Any language works via shebang.
   175→
   176→### Task arguments
   177→
   178→```toml
   179→[tasks.deploy]
   180→usage = '''
   181→arg "<environment>" help="Target environment" default="staging"
   182→flag "-v --verbose" help="Verbose output"
   183→'''
   184→run = "deploy.sh ${usage_environment}"
   185→```
   186→
   187→## Namespace Taxonomy
   188→
   189→Name tasks as `namespace:verb`. The namespace is a domain noun, the verb is an action. This groups related tasks alphabetically in `mise tasks` output.
   190→
   191→```toml
   192→# Per-project namespaces use the project's domain
   193→tasks."vault:triage" = "claude --print /inbox-process"
   194→tasks."cluster:deploy" = "talosctl apply-config"
   195→
   196→# Cross-project namespaces for shared concerns
   197→tasks."cc:prime" = "claude --print /vault-prime"
   198→tasks."git:clean" = "git branch --merged | grep -v main | xargs git branch -d"
   199→```
   200→
   201→Use the project's primary domain noun as the namespace. Avoid generic names like `run:` or `do:`.
   202→
   203→## Environment Variables
   204→
   205→```toml
   206→[env]
   207→NODE_ENV = "development"
   208→PROJECT_ROOT = "{{config_root}}"
   209→
   210→# Load from dotenv
   211→_.file = ".env"
   212→
   213→# Python virtual environment
   214→_.python.venv = { path = ".venv", create = true }
   215→```
   216→
   217→Template variables: `{{config_root}}` (directory containing mise.toml), `{{env.VAR}}` (existing env vars).
   218→
   219→## Layered Configuration
   220→
   221→mise resolves configuration from multiple levels, with closer files taking precedence:
   222→
   223→```text
   224→~/.config/mise/config.toml       # Global: cross-cutting tools and tasks
   225→./mise.toml                      # Project: local tools and tasks
   226→./mise.local.toml                # Local overrides (gitignored)
   227→```
   228→
   229→Global config handles cross-cutting concerns (shared tools, repo orchestration tasks). Project config handles repo-specific tools and tasks. Global tasks never assume a specific repo structure — use environment variables or auto-detection.
   230→
   231→Use `mise.local.toml` (gitignored) for environment-specific overrides. Tasks can generate this file to switch environments:
   232→
   233→```toml
   234→[tasks."env:local"]
   235→description = "Switch to local LAN environment"
   236→run = '''
   237→cat > .mise.local.toml << 'EOF'
   238→[env]
   239→NOMAD_ADDR = "http://192.168.11.11:4646"
   240→CONSUL_HTTP_ADDR = "http://192.168.11.11:8500"
   241→EOF
   242→echo "Switched to local. Run 'mise trust' to apply."
   243→'''
   244→```
   245→
   246→## Task Dependencies
   247→
   248→```toml
   249→[tasks.check]
   250→description = "Full quality gate"
   251→depends = ["format:check", "lint", "test"]
   252→
   253→[tasks.deploy]
   254→depends = ["check"]
   255→depends_post = ["notify"]           # Runs after this task completes
   256→wait_for = ["build"]                # Waits without forcing execution
   257→```
   258→
   259→Independent dependencies run in parallel by default. Control parallelism with `[settings] jobs = 4`.
   260→
   261→## Settings
   262→
   263→```toml
   264→[settings]
   265→experimental = true                 # Required for file-based tasks
   266→jobs = 4                            # Parallel task execution
   267→task_output = "prefix"              # prefix | interleave | quiet | silent
   268→```
   269→
   270→Language-specific settings nest under the tool name: `python.compile`, `rust.cargo_home`. See language reference files for details.
   271→
   272→## Claude Code Integration
   273→
   274→mise tasks orchestrate shell-side operations around Claude Code sessions. The pattern: mise owns the shell, Claude owns the conversation. When a mise task needs Claude, invoke the CLI with `claude --print`. Reserve interactive `claude` for workflows requiring user interaction. Never reimplement slash command logic in mise — wrap it.
   275→
   276→See [Examples](examples/examples.md) for complete Claude Code integration task patterns.
   277→
   278→## Reference Guides
   279→
   280→- [Python Ecosystem](references/python.md) — Version management, venv handling, and task patterns for Python projects
   281→- [Rust Ecosystem](references/rust.md) — Toolchain management and task patterns for Rust projects
   282→- [Examples](examples/examples.md) — Complete `mise.toml` files for common project types
   283→
   284→## External Resources
   285→
   286→- [mise documentation](https://mise.jdx.dev/) — Official docs
   287→- [mise GitHub](https://github.com/jdx/mise) — Source repository
   288→- [mise.toml schema](https://mise.jdx.dev/schema/mise.json) — JSON schema for editor validation
   289→

1→# mise: Rust Ecosystem
     2→
     3→Rust toolchain management and common task patterns for Rust projects.
     4→
     5→## Rust Toolchain Management
     6→
     7→mise manages Rust through rustup. Installations live in rustup's directory structure, not mise's. mise sets `RUSTUP_TOOLCHAIN` to activate the correct version.
     8→
     9→```toml
    10→[tools]
    11→rust = "stable"               # Most common
    12→```
    13→
    14→Other channel options:
    15→
    16→```toml
    17→[tools]
    18→rust = "1.83"                 # Pinned version
    19→# or
    20→rust = "nightly"              # Nightly channel
    21→```
    22→
    23→### Components and profiles
    24→
    25→```toml
    26→[tools]
    27→rust = {
    28→  version = "stable",
    29→  components = "clippy,rustfmt,rust-src",
    30→  profile = "default"
    31→}
    32→```
    33→
    34→Profile options: `minimal` (compiler only), `default` (plus docs, rustfmt, clippy), `complete` (all components).
    35→
    36→### Cross-compilation targets
    37→
    38→```toml
    39→[tools]
    40→rust = {
    41→  version = "stable",
    42→  targets = "wasm32-unknown-unknown,aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu"
    43→}
    44→```
    45→
    46→### When to use mise vs rustup directly
    47→
    48→Use mise for version pinning in `mise.toml` — the project declares which Rust version it needs, and mise activates it. Rustup handles the underlying installation, component management, and target configuration. The two work together: mise selects, rustup provides.
    49→
    50→### Rust settings
    51→
    52→```toml
    53→[settings]
    54→rust.cargo_home = "~/.cargo"
    55→rust.rustup_home = "~/.rustup"
    56→```
    57→
    58→These isolate mise's Rust installation from other rustup setups on the system when needed.
    59→
    60→## Task Patterns
    61→
    62→### Building
    63→
    64→```toml
    65→[tasks.build]
    66→description = "Build in debug mode"
    67→run = "cargo build"
    68→
    69→[tasks."build:release"]
    70→description = "Build optimized release binary"
    71→run = "cargo build --release"
    72→sources = ["src/**/*.rs", "Cargo.toml", "Cargo.lock"]
    73→outputs = ["target/release/{{env.CARGO_BIN_NAME}}"]
    74→```
    75→
    76→### Testing
    77→
    78→```toml
    79→[tasks.test]
    80→description = "Run test suite"
    81→run = "cargo test"
    82→
    83→[tasks."test:verbose"]
    84→description = "Run tests with output"
    85→run = "cargo test -- --nocapture"
    86→
    87→[tasks."test:doc"]
    88→description = "Run documentation tests"
    89→run = "cargo test --doc"
    90→```
    91→
    92→### Linting and Formatting
    93→
    94→```toml
    95→[tasks.lint]
    96→description = "Run clippy with strict warnings"
    97→run = "cargo clippy -- -D warnings"
    98→
    99→[tasks.format]
   100→description = "Format code"
   101→run = "cargo fmt"
   102→
   103→[tasks."format:check"]
   104→description = "Check formatting without changes"
   105→run = "cargo fmt -- --check"
   106→```
   107→
   108→### Quality Gate
   109→
   110→```toml
   111→[tasks.check]
   112→description = "Full quality gate"
   113→depends = ["format:check", "lint", "test"]
   114→```
   115→
   116→### Documentation
   117→
   118→```toml
   119→[tasks.doc]
   120→description = "Build and open docs"
   121→run = "cargo doc --open --no-deps"
   122→
   123→[tasks."doc:check"]
   124→description = "Check doc warnings"
   125→run = "RUSTDOCFLAGS='-D warnings' cargo doc --no-deps"
   126→```
   127→
   128→### Development Workflow
   129→
   130→```toml
   131→[tasks.watch]
   132→description = "Rebuild on file changes"
   133→run = "cargo watch -x build"
   134→
   135→[tasks.run]
   136→description = "Run the binary"
   137→depends = ["build"]
   138→run = "cargo run"
   139→
   140→[tasks.install]
   141→description = "Install binary locally"
   142→run = "cargo install --path ."
   143→```
   144→
   145→### Workspace Tasks
   146→
   147→For Cargo workspaces with multiple crates:
   148→
   149→```toml
   150→[tasks."ws:test"]
   151→description = "Test all workspace members"
   152→run = "cargo test --workspace"
   153→
   154→[tasks."ws:check"]
   155→description = "Check all workspace members"
   156→run = "cargo check --workspace"
   157→```
   158→
   159→## Cross-Compilation
   160→
   161→Set target-specific environment variables through `[env]`:
   162→
   163→```toml
   164→[env]
   165→CARGO_TARGET_AARCH64_UNKNOWN_LINUX_GNU_LINKER = "aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc"
   166→
   167→[tasks."build:arm64"]
   168→description = "Cross-compile for ARM64 Linux"
   169→run = "cargo build --release --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu"
   170→```
   171→
   172→For WASM targets:
   173→
   174→```toml
   175→[tasks."build:wasm"]
   176→description = "Build for WASM"
   177→run = "cargo build --release --target wasm32-unknown-unknown"
   178→```
   179→
   180→## Cargo Tool Extensions
   181→
   182→Install additional Cargo tools through mise backends:
   183→
   184→```toml
   185→[tools]
   186→"cargo:cargo-watch" = "latest"
   187→"cargo:cargo-nextest" = "latest"
   188→"cargo:cargo-audit" = "latest"
   189→```
   190→
   191→These install as mise-managed tools, separate from the project's `Cargo.toml` dependencies.
   192→

1→# mise: Python Ecosystem
     2→
     3→Python version management, virtual environment handling, and common task patterns for Python projects.
     4→
     5→## Python Version Management
     6→
     7→mise manages Python versions through its core plugin. No separate plugin install required.
     8→
     9→```toml
    10→[tools]
    11→python = "3.12"               # Latest 3.12.x (prefix match)
    12→```
    13→
    14→Other version formats:
    15→
    16→```toml
    17→[tools]
    18→python = "3.12.4"             # Exact version
    19→# or
    20→python = ["3.11", "3.12"]     # Multiple versions (first is default)
    21→```
    22→
    23→By default, mise installs precompiled Python binaries for speed. To compile from source (needed for custom build flags or uncommon platforms):
    24→
    25→```toml
    26→[settings]
    27→python.compile = true
    28→```
    29→
    30→### Idiomatic version files
    31→
    32→mise reads `.python-version` files when idiomatic version file support is enabled, providing compatibility with pyenv-based workflows.
    33→
    34→## Virtual Environments
    35→
    36→Configure automatic venv creation and activation through the `[env]` section:
    37→
    38→```toml
    39→[env]
    40→_.python.venv = { path = ".venv", create = true }
    41→```
    42→
    43→This creates `.venv` if it doesn't exist and activates it when entering the project directory.
    44→
    45→### Extended options
    46→
    47→```toml
    48→[env]
    49→_.python.venv = {
    50→  path = ".venv",
    51→  create = true,
    52→  python = "3.11",                          # Pin venv to specific version
    53→  uv_create_args = ["--seed"]               # Pass args to uv venv
    54→}
    55→```
    56→
    57→### UV integration
    58→
    59→To align UV's Python resolution with the mise-managed version:
    60→
    61→```toml
    62→[env]
    63→UV_PYTHON = { value = "{{ tools.python.path }}", tools = true }
    64→```
    65→
    66→### Settings
    67→
    68→```toml
    69→[settings]
    70→python.uv_venv_auto = false                # false | "source" | "create|source" | true
    71→python.venv_stdlib = false                  # Use stdlib venv instead of uv/virtualenv
    72→```
    73→
    74→## uv Integration
    75→
    76→Most Python projects use uv as the package manager. Prefix commands with `uv run` to execute through the managed venv:
    77→
    78→```toml
    79→[tasks.python-install]
    80→description = "Install Python dependencies"
    81→run = "uv sync"
    82→
    83→[tasks.python-upgrade]
    84→description = "Upgrade Python dependencies"
    85→run = "uv lock --upgrade"
    86→
    87→[tasks.python-clean]
    88→description = "Clean development environment"
    89→run = ["rm -rf .venv", "rm -rf requirements.lock"]
    90→```
    91→
    92→For CI, use `uv sync --frozen` to install from the lockfile without updating it.
    93→
    94→## Task Patterns
    95→
    96→All task commands below assume uv-managed projects. Prefix with `uv run` when executing tools installed in the project venv.
    97→
    98→### Testing
    99→
   100→```toml
   101→[tasks.test]
   102→description = "Run test suite"
   103→run = "uv run pytest"
   104→
   105→[tasks."test:coverage"]
   106→description = "Run tests with coverage"
   107→run = "uv run pytest --cov=src --cov-report=term-missing"
   108→
   109→[tasks."test:watch"]
   110→description = "Run tests on file change"
   111→run = "uv run ptw -- -x"
   112→```
   113→
   114→### Linting and Formatting
   115→
   116→```toml
   117→[tasks.lint]
   118→description = "Check code style"
   119→run = "uv run ruff check ."
   120→
   121→[tasks."lint:fix"]
   122→description = "Auto-fix lint issues"
   123→run = "uv run ruff check --fix ."
   124→
   125→[tasks.format]
   126→description = "Format code"
   127→run = "uv run ruff format ."
   128→
   129→[tasks."format:check"]
   130→description = "Check formatting without changes"
   131→run = "uv run ruff format --check ."
   132→```
   133→
   134→### Type Checking
   135→
   136→```toml
   137→[tasks.typecheck]
   138→description = "Run type checker"
   139→run = "uv run mypy src/"
   140→```
   141→
   142→### Quality Gate
   143→
   144→```toml
   145→[tasks.check]
   146→description = "Full quality gate"
   147→depends = ["format:check", "lint", "typecheck", "test"]
   148→```
   149→
   150→### Packaging
   151→
   152→```toml
   153→[tasks.build]
   154→description = "Build distribution packages"
   155→depends = ["check"]
   156→run = "uv run python -m build"
   157→
   158→[tasks.publish]
   159→description = "Publish to PyPI"
   160→depends = ["build"]
   161→run = "uv run twine upload dist/*"
   162→```
   163→
   164→### Development Server
   165→
   166→```toml
   167→[tasks.serve]
   168→description = "Run development server"
   169→run = "uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000"
   170→
   171→[tasks."db:migrate"]
   172→description = "Run database migrations"
   173→run = "uv run alembic upgrade head"
   174→
   175→[tasks."db:revision"]
   176→description = "Create new migration"
   177→usage = 'arg "<message>" help="Migration description"'
   178→run = "uv run alembic revision --autogenerate -m \"$1\""
   179→```
   180→
   181→## Default Packages
   182→
   183→Create `~/.default-python-packages` with one package per line to auto-install into every new Python version:
   184→
   185→```text
   186→pip
   187→setuptools
   188→wheel
   189→pipx
   190→```
   191→
   192→Set `MISE_PYTHON_DEFAULT_PACKAGES_FILE` to use a custom path.
   193→

1→# mise: Examples
     2→
     3→Complete `mise.toml` files for common project types. Copy and adapt as needed.
     4→
     5→## Common Base Tasks
     6→
     7→Standard boilerplate tasks that appear across most projects. Include the relevant subset when onboarding a repo.
     8→
     9→```toml
    10→min_version = "2024.9.5"
    11→
    12→[settings]
    13→auto_install = true
    14→not_found_auto_install = true
    15→task_run_auto_install = true
    16→
    17→[settings.status]
    18→show_tools = true
    19→show_env = false
    20→truncate = true
    21→
    22→[tools]
    23→git-cliff = "2.10.1"
    24→prek = "0.2.20"
    25→"pipx:rumdl" = "latest"
    26→
    27→# === Changelog ===
    28→[tasks.changelog]
    29→description = "Update changelog using git-cliff"
    30→run = "git-cliff -o CHANGELOG.md"
    31→
    32→# === Hooks ===
    33→[tasks.hooks-install]
    34→description = "Install pre-commit and infisical hooks"
    35→run = "prek install -f && infisical scan install --pre-commit-hook"
    36→
    37→[tasks.pre-commit-run]
    38→description = "Run pre-commit hooks"
    39→run = "prek run --all-files"
    40→
    41→# === Security ===
    42→[tasks.infisical-scan]
    43→description = "Scan repository for secrets"
    44→run = "infisical scan"
    45→
    46→# === Markdown ===
    47→[tasks.markdown-lint]
    48→description = "Lint Markdown files"
    49→run = "rumdl check ."
    50→
    51→[tasks.markdown-fix]
    52→description = "Fix Markdown files"
    53→run = "rumdl fmt ."
    54→
    55→# === Composite ===
    56→[tasks.setup]
    57→description = "Set up development environment"
    58→depends = ["hooks-install"]
    59→```
    60→
    61→## Minimal Starter
    62→
    63→Bare-minimum configuration for any project. Add tools and tasks as the project grows.
    64→
    65→```toml
    66→[tools]
    67→node = "22"
    68→
    69→[tasks]
    70→dev = "npm run dev"
    71→test = "npm test"
    72→```
    73→
    74→## Python Web Application
    75→
    76→Full configuration for a Python web project with FastAPI, database migrations, and standard quality tooling.
    77→
    78→```toml
    79→[env]
    80→DATABASE_URL = "postgres://localhost/myapp_dev"
    81→_.python.venv = { path = ".venv", create = true }
    82→
    83→[tools]
    84→python = "3.12"
    85→
    86→[tasks]
    87→serve.run = "uvicorn app.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000"
    88→serve.description = "Run development server"
    89→
    90→test.run = "pytest"
    91→test.description = "Run test suite"
    92→
    93→"test:coverage".run = "pytest --cov=src --cov-report=term-missing"
    94→"test:coverage".description = "Run tests with coverage"
    95→
    96→lint.run = "ruff check ."
    97→lint.description = "Check code style"
    98→
    99→"lint:fix".run = "ruff check --fix ."
   100→format.run = "ruff format ."
   101→typecheck.run = "mypy src/"
   102→
   103→[tasks.check]
   104→description = "Full quality gate"
   105→depends = ["lint", "typecheck", "test"]
   106→
   107→[tasks."db:migrate"]
   108→description = "Run database migrations"
   109→run = "alembic upgrade head"
   110→
   111→[tasks."db:revision"]
   112→description = "Create new migration"
   113→usage = 'arg "<message>" help="Migration description"'
   114→run = "alembic revision --autogenerate -m \"$1\""
   115→
   116→[settings]
   117→experimental = true
   118→```
   119→
   120→## Rust CLI Tool
   121→
   122→Configuration for a Rust CLI project with standard Cargo workflows and source tracking.
   123→
   124→```toml
   125→[env]
   126→RUST_LOG = "info"
   127→
   128→[tools]
   129→rust = { version = "stable", components = "clippy,rustfmt" }
   130→"cargo:cargo-watch" = "latest"
   131→
   132→[tasks]
   133→build.run = "cargo build"
   134→build.description = "Build in debug mode"
   135→
   136→"build:release".run = "cargo build --release"
   137→"build:release".description = "Build optimized release binary"
   138→"build:release".sources = ["src/**/*.rs", "Cargo.toml", "Cargo.lock"]
   139→
   140→test.run = "cargo test"
   141→test.description = "Run test suite"
   142→
   143→lint.run = "cargo clippy -- -D warnings"
   144→lint.description = "Run clippy with strict warnings"
   145→
   146→format.run = "cargo fmt"
   147→"format:check".run = "cargo fmt -- --check"
   148→
   149→watch.run = "cargo watch -x build"
   150→watch.description = "Rebuild on file changes"
   151→
   152→run.run = "cargo run"
   153→run.depends = ["build"]
   154→
   155→install.run = "cargo install --path ."
   156→
   157→[tasks.check]
   158→description = "Full quality gate"
   159→depends = ["format:check", "lint", "test"]
   160→
   161→[settings]
   162→experimental = true
   163→```
   164→
   165→## Multi-Language Project
   166→
   167→Python backend with Node.js tooling (documentation site, pre-commit hooks, or frontend build).
   168→
   169→```toml
   170→[env]
   171→_.python.venv = { path = ".venv", create = true }
   172→
   173→[tools]
   174→python = "3.12"
   175→node = "22"
   176→
   177→[tasks]

     4→
     5→# Skill Development Standards
     6→
     7→## SKILL.md Structure
     8→
     9→Every skill requires a `SKILL.md` file with:
    10→
    11→1. **Title** - Clear, descriptive name
    12→2. **Description** - What the skill provides
    13→3. **Triggers** - Concrete activation conditions
    14→4. **Instructions** - Step-by-step guidance
    15→5. **References** - Links to supporting files
    16→
    17→## Skill Directory Layout
    18→
    19→```text
    20→skills/<skill-name>/
    21→├── SKILL.md              # Main skill definition (required)
    22→├── references/           # Reference documentation
    23→├── examples/             # Example code/configs
    24→├── patterns/             # Reusable patterns
    25→├── anti-patterns/        # Common mistakes to avoid
    26→├── tools/                # Helper scripts
    27→└── workflows/            # Multi-step procedures
    28→```
    29→
    30→## Trigger Patterns
    31→
    32→Triggers should be **concrete and actionable**, not vague:
    33→
    34→**Good triggers:**
    35→
    36→- "When creating a new Ansible role"
    37→- "When configuring Proxmox cloud-init templates"
    38→- "When writing PEP 723 inline script metadata"
    39→
    40→**Bad triggers:**
    41→
    42→- "When working with infrastructure"
    43→- "When needed"
    44→- "For Python tasks"
    45→
    46→## Reference Organization
    47→
    48→- Keep references focused and specific
    49→- Use markdown files for documentation
    50→- Include working code examples
    51→- Document anti-patterns explicitly
    52→
    53→## CRITICAL: Dynamic Bash Pattern Bug (GitHub #12762)
    54→
    55→The skill parser executes dynamic bash patterns even inside fenced code blocks.
    56→
    57→**NEVER use exclamation-backtick patterns in SKILL.md code examples.** The parser executes them during skill load. Escaping with backslash does NOT work.
    58→
    59→**Use `$` shell notation instead:**
    60→
    61→```text
    62→# GOOD - Safe in code blocks:
    63→$ git status
    64→$ bash ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/script.sh
    65→```
    66→
    67→**Workarounds:**
    68→
    69→1. Use `$ command` notation in examples
    70→2. Describe syntax in prose rather than showing literal patterns
    71→3. Move examples to reference files (not parsed as skill content)
    72→
    73→This also applies to `@` file reference patterns in code blocks.
    74→

## Skill Review: mise

### Summary

The mise skill is a comprehensive guide to the mise polyglot tool version manager and task runner. It covers configuration structure, tool versions, task definitions, environment variables, layered configuration, and Claude Code integration. The skill uses progressive disclosure effectively, delegating language-specific detail and complete examples to supporting files. The description is well-constructed with strong trigger phrases. This is a high-quality skill with a few areas for tightening.

Word counts (approximate):

- SKILL.md body: ~1,950 words
- `references/python.md`: ~850 words
- `references/rust.md`: ~700 words
- `examples/examples.md`: ~1,050 words
- Total ecosystem: ~4,550 words

### Description Analysis

Current description:

> This skill provides mise configuration patterns, task definitions, and language-specific setup guidance. This skill should be used when the user asks to "set up mise", "create a mise.toml", "configure task runner", "manage tool versions", "add a task to mise", "set up Python with mise", "configure Rust toolchain", "onboard a repo", "add mise to this project", "set up dev environment", or mentions mise configuration, dev tooling setup, task definitions, or project onboarding.

Issues:

- The description is 402 characters, which sits comfortably within the recommended range.
- Uses correct third-person phrasing ("This skill should be used when...").
- Trigger phrases are concrete and specific. Good coverage of both mise-specific queries ("create a mise.toml") and intent-based queries ("onboard a repo", "set up dev environment").
- Minor gap: no trigger for environment variable configuration ("set up env vars in mise") or layered/global config queries ("configure global mise", "set up mise.local.toml").

Recommendations:

- Consider adding two more trigger phrases to close the gaps on environment and layered config use cases. Suggested improved description:

> This skill provides mise configuration patterns, task definitions, and language-specific setup guidance. This skill should be used when the user asks to "set up mise", "create a mise.toml", "configure task runner", "manage tool versions", "add a task to mise", "set up Python with mise", "configure Rust toolchain", "onboard a repo", "add mise to this project", "set up dev environment", "configure environment variables in mise", "set up global mise config", or mentions mise configuration, dev tooling setup, task definitions, layered configuration, or project onboarding.

This adds 48 characters bringing it to ~450, still under the 500-character guideline.

### Content Quality

SKILL.md Analysis:

- Word count: ~1,950 words (well within the 1,000-3,000 recommended range)
- Writing style: Predominantly imperative/infinitive. Good. Sentences like "Order sections canonically for consistency", "Specify tools in the [tools] section", "Name tasks as namespace:verb" are clean directive prose.
- Organization: Logical progression from config structure to tools to tasks to environment to layered config to integration. Sections are well-scoped and build on each other.

Issues:

1. Line 14: The opening sentence is descriptive/expository rather than imperative. "mise is a polyglot tool version manager and task runner" is fine as orientation, but the second sentence ("It manages tool versions, defines project tasks, and sets environment variables from one configuration file") is somewhat redundant with the description frontmatter. This is minor.

2. Lines 187-199, Namespace Taxonomy section: The example tasks reference specific operator tooling (`vault:triage`, `cluster:deploy`, `cc:prime`). These are useful for the operator's personal context but could confuse a general consumer of the skill. The guidance itself ("Name tasks as namespace:verb") is excellent and universal.

3. Line 264, Settings section: The `experimental = true` setting is noted as "Required for file-based tasks." This may be outdated. As of recent mise versions, file-based tasks no longer require the experimental flag. Worth verifying against current mise docs and removing if stale.

4. Lines 272-276, Claude Code Integration section: Good bridge content. The principle "mise owns the shell, Claude owns the conversation" is clear and useful. However, it references `examples/examples.md` with a relative link ("See [Examples](examples/examples.md)") -- this is fine structurally, but the actual examples file's Claude Code section (lines 274-308 of `examples/examples.md`) uses `claude --print "$1"` with double quotes, which may not work as expected in all shells when invoked through mise. This is a minor correctness concern in the examples file, not SKILL.md itself.

Recommendations:

- Verify whether `experimental = true` is still required for file-based tasks in current mise versions. If deprecated, remove the note at line 265 and the corresponding `[settings] experimental = true` lines in the examples.
- The Namespace Taxonomy section is strong. No changes needed; the operator-specific examples actually serve as good concrete illustrations even if domain-specific.

### Progressive Disclosure

Current Structure:

- `SKILL.md`: ~1,950 words (core guidance)
- `references/`: 2 files (~1,550 words total)
  - `python.md`: ~850 words
  - `rust.md`: ~700 words
- `examples/`: 1 file (~1,050 words)
  - `examples.md`: Complete mise.toml templates for 7 project types
- `scripts/`: 0 files

Assessment:

Progressive disclosure is well-implemented. The core SKILL.md covers mise fundamentals that apply regardless of language. Language-specific detail (Python venv handling, Rust cross-compilation, cargo tool extensions) is properly delegated to reference files. Complete copy-paste-ready configurations live in the examples file. The references are linked at the bottom of SKILL.md in a clear "Reference Guides" section (lines 279-282).

One structural observation: the `examples/examples.md` file includes a "Common Base Tasks" section (lines 7-59) that contains the operator's specific boilerplate (git-cliff, prek, rumdl, infisical). This is valuable for the operator's onboarding workflow but is opinionated. This is appropriate for a personal plugin marketplace and not an issue.

Recommendations:

- No major restructuring needed. The balance between core and reference content is good.
- If a Go or other language ecosystem is added in the future, the pattern of `references/<language>.md` scales naturally.

### Specific Issues

#### Critical (0)

None.

#### Major (1)

1. `/Users/basher8383/3I/workshop/lunar-claude/plugins/devops/repo-forge/skills/mise/SKILL.md` line 265: The statement "Required for file-based tasks" regarding `experimental = true` may be outdated. Recent mise versions (2024.11+) graduated file-based tasks from experimental. If this is stale, it will cause unnecessary configuration in generated files. Verify against current mise docs and update accordingly. The same setting appears in `examples/examples.md` at lines 117, 161, 192, and 292.

#### Minor (3)

1. `/Users/basher8383/3I/workshop/lunar-claude/plugins/devops/repo-forge/skills/mise/SKILL.md` lines 279-282: The reference links section lists "Examples" twice in slightly different ways (as a bullet item and implicitly through the section heading). The third bullet duplicates info from the second reference link. Consider whether both the "Reference Guides" bullets and the "External Resources" section are both needed, or if the reference guides section could be streamlined to just the two language references since examples are already mentioned in the Claude Code Integration section above it.

## Settings

```toml
[settings]
experimental = true                 # Required for file-based tasks
jobs = 4                            # Parallel task execution
task_output = "prefix"              # prefix | interleave | quiet | silent
```

Language-specific settings nest under the tool name: `python.compile`, `rust.cargo_home`. See language reference files for details.

## Claude Code Integration

mise tasks orchestrate shell-side operations around Claude Code sessions. The pattern: mise owns the shell, Claude owns the conversation. When a mise task needs Claude, invoke the CLI with `claude --print`. Reserve interactive `claude` for workflows requiring user interaction. Never reimplement slash command logic in mise — wrap it.

file.content:
description = "Initial project setup"
depends = ["docs"]
```

## Claude Code Integration

Tasks that wrap Claude Code CLI for AI-assisted workflows. Add to a project's `mise.toml` or a global `~/.config/mise/config.toml`.

```toml
[tasks."cc:ask"]
description = "One-shot question to Claude about this project"
run = "claude --print \"$1\""

[tasks."cc:review"]
description = "AI review of staged changes"
run = "git diff --staged | claude --print 'Review these changes for bugs, style issues, and security concerns.'"

[tasks."cc:explain"]
description = "Explain a file using Claude"
usage = 'arg "<file>" help="File to explain"'
run = "cat \"$1\" | claude --print 'Explain this file. Focus on architecture and key decisions.'"

[settings]
experimental = true
```

For global cross-repo tasks, place in `~/.config/mise/config.toml`:

```toml
[tasks."repo:status"]
description = "Show git state of all managed repos"
run = '''
for repo in ~/dev/projects/*/; do
  if [ -d "$repo/.git" ]; then
    printf "%-30s %s\n" "$(basename $repo)" "$(git -C $repo status --short | head -1)"
  fi
done
'''
```

# mise

mise is a polyglot tool version manager and task runner. A single `mise.toml` replaces Makefiles, shell scripts, and language-specific version managers (nvm, pyenv, rustup). It manages tool versions, defines project tasks, and sets environment variables from one configuration file.

## Config File Structure

Order sections canonically for consistency:

```toml
min_version = "2024.9.5"

[env]
DATABASE_URL = "postgres://localhost/myapp_dev"

[tools]
python = "3.12"

[tasks]
test.run = "pytest"

[settings]
auto_install = true
not_found_auto_install = true
task_run_auto_install = true
```

Use `min_version` to enforce a minimum mise version. Recognized top-level sections: `[env]`, `[tools]`, `[tasks]`, `[settings]`, `[plugins]`, `[tool_alias]`, `[task_config]`, `[vars]`.

## Tool Version Management

Specify tools in the `[tools]` section. mise installs and activates them automatically.

```toml
[tools]
node = "22"                   # Major version prefix
python = "3.12.2"             # Exact version
go = "latest"                 # Latest stable
erlang = "lts"                # LTS channel
```

Multiple versions install side-by-side (first is default):

```toml
[tools]
python = ["3.11", "3.12"]
```

### Version formats

| Format | Example | Behavior |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Exact | `"3.12.2"` | Specific version |
| Prefix | `"3.12"` | Latest matching 3.12.x |
| Channel | `"latest"`, `"lts"` | Resolved at install time |
| Reference | `"ref:main"` | Git ref, compiled from source |
| Subtraction | `"sub-1:latest"` | One major behind latest |

### Table form with options

```toml
[tools]
node = { version = "22", postinstall = "corepack enable" }
rust = { version = "stable", components = "clippy,rustfmt", profile = "default" }
```

### Backend syntax for non-core tools

```toml
[tools]
"ubi:astral-sh/ruff" = "latest"     # GitHub binary releases
"cargo:cargo-watch" = "latest"       # Cargo crate
"npm:prettier" = "3"                 # npm package
"pipx:ansible-lint" = "latest"       # Python CLI tools via pipx
```

### Custom plugins

Register custom tool plugins in `[plugins]`:

```toml
[plugins]
fnox-env = "https://github.com/jdx/mise-env-fnox"
```

## Task Definitions

Tasks are defined in `[tasks]` or as executable files in `.mise/tasks/`.

### Inline tasks

```toml
tasks.test = "pytest"
tasks.lint = "ruff check ."
tasks.format = "ruff format ."
```

### Table tasks

```toml
[tasks.build]
description = "Build the project"
depends = ["lint"]
run = "cargo build --release"
dir = "{{config_root}}/backend"
env = { RUST_LOG = "info" }
sources = ["src/**/*.rs", "Cargo.toml"]
outputs = ["target/release/myapp"]
```

### Task-level options

Pin tool versions per-task, set aliases for shortcuts, or override the shell:

```toml
[tasks."lint:shellcheck"]
description = "Lint shell scripts"
run = "shellcheck scripts/*.sh"
tools = { "shellcheck" = "0.11.0" }    # Pin tool version for this task
alias = "sc"                            # Short alias for mise run sc
shell = "bash -c"                       # Override default shell
```

### Task directory scoping

Use `dir` to run a task in a specific subdirectory. Essential for monorepos and infrastructure projects where different tasks target different paths:

```toml
[tasks.prod-validate]
description = "Validate production Terraform"
dir = "infrastructure/environments/production"
run = "terraform init -backend=false -input=false >/dev/null && terraform validate"

[tasks.prod-plan]
description = "Plan production changes"
dir = "infrastructure/environments/production"
run = "terraform plan"
```

Relative paths resolve from the config file's directory. Use `{{config_root}}` for explicit anchoring. When multiple tasks share a base directory, the pattern of environment-scoped tasks (same command, different `dir`) keeps configs DRY while remaining explicit about scope.

### Multi-line scripts

```toml
[tasks.setup]
run = [
  "pip install -e '.[dev]'",
  "pre-commit install"
]
```

### File-based tasks

Place executable scripts in `.mise/tasks/`. Subdirectories create namespaces — `.mise/tasks/db/migrate` becomes task `db:migrate`.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#MISE description="Run database migrations"
#MISE depends=["db:check"]

alembic upgrade head
```

Mark file tasks executable (`chmod +x`). Any language works via shebang.

### Task arguments

```toml
[tasks.deploy]
usage = '''
arg "<environment>" help="Target environment" default="staging"
flag "-v --verbose" help="Verbose output"
'''
run = "deploy.sh ${usage_environment}"
```

## Namespace Taxonomy

Name tasks as `namespace:verb`. The namespace is a domain noun, the verb is an action. This groups related tasks alphabetically in `mise tasks` output.

```toml
# Per-project namespaces use the project's domain
tasks."vault:triage" = "claude --print /inbox-process"
tasks."cluster:deploy" = "talosctl apply-config"

# Cross-project namespaces for shared concerns
tasks."cc:prime" = "claude --print /vault-prime"
tasks."git:clean" = "git branch --merged | grep -v main | xargs git branch -d"
```

Use the project's primary domain noun as the namespace. Avoid generic names like `run:` or `do:`.

## Environment Variables

```toml
[env]
NODE_ENV = "development"
PROJECT_ROOT = "{{config_root}}"

# Load from dotenv
_.file = ".env"

# Python virtual environment
_.python.venv = { path = ".venv", create = true }
```

Template variables: `{{config_root}}` (directory containing mise.toml), `{{env.VAR}}` (existing env vars).

## Layered Configuration

mise resolves configuration from multiple levels, with closer files taking precedence:

```text
~/.config/mise/config.toml       # Global: cross-cutting tools and tasks
./mise.toml                      # Project: local tools and tasks
./mise.local.toml                # Local overrides (gitignored)
```

Global config handles cross-cutting concerns (shared tools, repo orchestration tasks). Project config handles repo-specific tools and tasks. Global tasks never assume a specific repo structure — use environment variables or auto-detection.

Use `mise.local.toml` (gitignored) for environment-specific overrides. Tasks can generate this file to switch environments:

```toml
[tasks."env:local"]
description = "Switch to local LAN environment"
run = '''
cat > .mise.local.toml << 'EOF'
[env]
NOMAD_ADDR = "http://192.168.11.11:4646"
CONSUL_HTTP_ADDR = "http://192.168.11.11:8500"
EOF
echo "Switched to local. Run 'mise trust' to apply."
'''
```

## Task Dependencies

```toml
[tasks.check]
description = "Full quality gate"
depends = ["format:check", "lint", "test"]

[tasks.deploy]
depends = ["check"]
depends_post = ["notify"]           # Runs after this task completes
wait_for = ["build"]                # Waits without forcing execution
```

Independent dependencies run in parallel by default. Control parallelism with `[settings] jobs = 4`.

## Settings

```toml
[settings]
experimental = true                 # Required for file-based tasks
jobs = 4                            # Parallel task execution
task_output = "prefix"              # prefix | interleave | quiet | silent
```

Language-specific settings nest under the tool name: `python.compile`, `rust.cargo_home`. See language reference files for details.

## Claude Code Integration

mise tasks orchestrate shell-side operations around Claude Code sessions. The pattern: mise owns the shell, Claude owns the conversation. When a mise task needs Claude, invoke the CLI with `claude --print`. Reserve interactive `claude` for workflows requiring user interaction. Never reimplement slash command logic in mise — wrap it.

See [Examples](examples/examples.md) for complete Claude Code integration task patterns.

## Reference Guides

- [Python Ecosystem](references/python.md) — Version management, venv handling, and task patterns for Python projects
- [Rust Ecosystem](references/rust.md) — Toolchain management and task patterns for Rust projects
- [Examples](examples/examples.md) — Complete `mise.toml` files for common project types

## External Resources

- [mise documentation](https://mise.jdx.dev/) — Official docs
- [mise GitHub](https://github.com/jdx/mise) — Source repository
- [mise.toml schema](https://mise.jdx.dev/schema/mise.json) — JSON schema for editor validation

## Definition

According to the documentation, the `experimental` setting "Enables experimental features. I generally will publish new features under this config which needs to be enabled to use them."

The key points are:

- **Type**: boolean
- **Environment variable**: `MISE_EXPERIMENTAL`
- **Default**: `false`

## Features Requiring experimental = true

The documentation states: "While a feature is marked as 'experimental' its behavior may change or even disappear in any release."

However, **the documentation does not specify which particular features currently require this setting**. It only explains that experimental features may be iterated on to "get the behavior right" before the experimental label is removed.

## File-Based Tasks

There is **no mention** in this settings documentation of file-based tasks requiring the experimental flag. The task-related settings discussed (under the `task` section) do not reference an experimental requirement.

The documentation does mention that experimental mode can serve as a "beta flag" for testing new functionality with a smaller user subset, but it doesn't explicitly list which current features fall into this category."}]},"uuid":"ca5b6180-7de0-46ee-93e6-2bf89e86446b","timestamp":"2026-03-02T02:25:43.074Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"a7417cb2-3126-404b-95e7-6a909150d97d"}

Based on the search results, here's what I found about mise's file-based tasks in the experimental setting context for 2025-2026:

## Experimental Features for Tasks

Monorepo tasks require the MISE_EXPERIMENTAL=1 environment variable, making this an experimental feature. Comprehensive monorepo support for tasks landed in October 2025, which represents one of the major developments in this area.

## Key Experimental Settings

When using monorepo mode (experimental_monorepo_root = true), this controls how deep mise will search for task files in subdirectories. When experimental_monorepo_root is enabled in your root mise.toml, mise will automatically discover tasks in subdirectories and prefix them with their relative path from the monorepo root, creating a unified task namespace across your entire repository.

## Deprecation Timeline

There's an important deprecation notice: Using Tera template functions in run scripts is deprecated and will be removed in mise 2026.11.0, with versions >= 2026.5.0 showing a deprecation warning.

## Definition

According to the documentation, the `experimental` setting "Enables experimental features. I generally will publish new features under this config which needs to be enabled to use them."

The key points are:

- **Type**: boolean
- **Environment variable**: `MISE_EXPERIMENTAL`
- **Default**: `false`

## Features Requiring experimental = true

The documentation states: "While a feature is marked as 'experimental' its behavior may change or even disappear in any release."

However, **the documentation does not specify which particular features currently require this setting**. It only explains that experimental features may be iterated on to "get the behavior right" before the experimental label is removed.

## File-Based Tasks

There is **no mention** in this settings documentation of file-based tasks requiring the experimental flag. The task-related settings discussed (under the `task` section) do not reference an experimental requirement.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about mise's file-based tasks in the experimental setting context for 2025-2026:

## Experimental Features for Tasks

Monorepo tasks require the MISE_EXPERIMENTAL=1 environment variable, making this an experimental feature. Comprehensive monorepo support for tasks landed in October 2025, which represents one of the major developments in this area.

## Key Experimental Settings

When using monorepo mode (experimental_monorepo_root = true), this controls how deep mise will search for task files in subdirectories. When experimental_monorepo_root is enabled in your root mise.toml, mise will automatically discover tasks in subdirectories and prefix them with their relative path from the monorepo root, creating a unified task namespace across your entire repository.

## Deprecation Timeline

There's an important deprecation notice: Using Tera template functions in run scripts is deprecated and will be removed in mise 2026.11.0, with versions >= 2026.5.0 showing a deprecation warning.

The monorepo tasks feature appears to be the primary file-based tasks experimental setting currently in development for 2025-2026, with significant advances having been made throughout 2025.

- **Type**: boolean

## Features Requiring experimental = true

The documentation states: "While a feature is marked as 'experimental' its behavior may change or even disappear in any release."

However, **the documentation does not specify which particular features currently require this setting**. It only explains that experimental features may be iterated on to "get the behavior right" before the experimental label is removed.

## File-Based Tasks

There is **no mention** in this settings documentation of file-based tasks requiring the experimental flag. The task-related settings discussed (under the `task` section) do not reference an experimental requirement.
